title: Iran War Shows Adversaries Can Exploit Big Data, Too
gist: The U.S.-Iran war produced the first known case of a U.S. adversary using commercial location data (from ad-tech and data brokers) to track and target U.S. forces, exposing that the open-source and commercial data ecosystem Washington exploits is equally available to sophisticated nation-state adversaries—a national-security vulnerability rooted in unregulated data brokers.

themes.theme: Commercial data turned into a targeting weapon

insights.claim: CENTCOM received reports—the first known of their kind—of a U.S. adversary, likely Iran, using commercial location data (latitude, longitude, and device identifiers obtained via ad-tech and data brokers) to track and target U.S. forces in the Middle East.
insights.speaker: Justin Sherman
insights.so_what: Commercial data-broker data is now a demonstrated military targeting input, elevating regulatory and reputational risk for the data-broker and ad-tech industry.
insights.quote: CENTCOM said it “has received multiple threat reports concerning adversary exploitation of commercial location data to target or surveil US personnel in theater.”
insights.locator: More recently, a bipartisan letter from two senators

insights.claim: Chinese firms, some with PLA ties, began marketing capabilities to 'expose' U.S. military movements—MizarVision uses Western and Chinese data filtered through AI to catalogue activity at U.S. Middle East bases, track naval movements, and identify aircraft and missile-defense positions, and claimed it 'quickly identified' U.S. equipment locations and carrier refueling patterns.
insights.speaker: Justin Sherman
insights.so_what: A commercial Chinese intelligence-fusion industry built on open data plus AI already exists and can feed adversary governments—an under-radar geopolitical capability.
insights.quote: a mix of Western and Chinese data filtered through AI to catalogue activity at U.S. bases in the Middle East, track naval movements, and identify the position and number of specific aircraft and missile defense systems
insights.locator: Before the recent allegations about Iran came to light

themes.theme: The data-broker ecosystem as systemic risk

insights.claim: Thousands of data brokers aggregate and sell people's marital status, religion, sexual orientation, income, debts, health conditions, prescriptions, IP addresses, devices, travels, and real-time GPS locations—often thousands of data points per person with weak vetting and no consumer consent—and bad actors can purchase it outright or deceptively.
insights.speaker: Justin Sherman
insights.so_what: The porous, high-volume data-broker market is the supply channel adversaries exploit; regulatory crackdown is a live policy risk for the sector.
insights.quote: Some data brokers sell thousands of data points on a single person, often with horrendously weak background checks and vetting—almost always without any semblance of consumer consent.
insights.locator: Beyond what anyone with an internet connection can view

insights.claim: Gaps in consumer privacy laws—including exemptions left in for national-security reasons—can backfire and become national-security threats, because the more porous and expansive the commercial data ecosystem, the easier it is for adversaries to slip in under the radar.
insights.speaker: Justin Sherman
insights.so_what: Privacy deregulation has a hidden national-security cost; expect renewed pressure to regulate data brokers framed as security rather than privacy.
insights.quote: Gaps in consumer privacy laws, including those left in for national security reasons, can backfire on governments and become national security threats.
insights.locator: The first shift centers the privacy-national security intersection

insights.claim: The actors who can do the most damage with open and commercial data are sophisticated nation-states (Iran, China, Russia) that have the money, storage, compute, and analytical processes to turn raw data into decision-enabling intelligence—not hobbyists or cybercriminals.
insights.speaker: Justin Sherman
insights.so_what: Data-exposure mitigations aimed only at petty actors will fail against nation-states; the real threat model is state intelligence services, reshaping how data-security policy should be designed.
insights.quote: having the money and data storage to acquire it, computing power to process it, knowledge to contextualize it, and rigorous analytical processes to turn it into decision-enabling intelligence—that is an entirely different animal

insights.claim: U.S. war planners can no longer assume adversaries only have data they have hacked; commercial and open-source data about deployments, facilities, and personnel must be factored into operational planning to avoid botching covert or surprise actions.
insights.speaker: Justin Sherman
insights.so_what: Operational security now hinges on the commercial data footprint—an emerging requirement that drives demand for data-exposure reduction and counter-surveillance services.
insights.quote: Cybersecurity experts, counterintelligence professionals, and war planners cannot assume that the adversary only has access to data and information it has hacked
insights.locator: Third, the United States will need to account
